name: Smoky Joe's Texas Red Chili
recipeIngredient: 1/2 cup Chimayo ground New Mexican red chile, medium hot
1/3 cup Bueno brand ground New Mexican red chile, hot
2 squares Goya brand semi-sweet Mexican chocolate
recipeInstructions:
This is the chili I made for my friend Wat Hughe's 1998 bi-annual chili party.
This is a Texas style red chili. Texas chili has no tomatoes but more importantly NO BEANS! Some of the ingredients I used, like the chocolate and the Wyler's granulated chicken broth in lieu of salt, are non-traditional but I like the flavor it adds. I pretty much winged it during the preparation but kept track of what I did so I could write it down. I think this batch turned out pretty darn good.
1. Rub the beef roast thoroughly with a BBQ spice rub, such as Texas Two Step BBQ Rub for Beef from Texas Two Step Foods.
2. Wrap the beef tightly in plastic wrap and refrigerate overnight.
3. The next day, smoke the beef roast at 225 degrees Fahrenheit over pecan wood chunks for 3 hours.
4. Meanwhile, brown the beef bones in a large stock pot. Add water, celery, carrots (unpeeled), onions (unpeeled), garlic (whole), and bay leaves. Simmer on very low heat for 10 hours. Strain the broth, removing the bones and vegetables.
5. Dice the beef roast into 1/2-inch cubes. Brown in a large cast iron Dutch oven or chili pot. Add a little vegetable oil if necessary. Brown the beef in small batches, covering the bottom of the pot in a single layer. After the last batch is browned, remove from the pot and add the chopped onion and garlic. Stir until the onions are soft and lightly brown. Return the beef to the pot.
6. Add the chili powder, ground red chile, paprika, cumin, and oregano. Add 8 cups of beef broth and bring to a simmer. Add the chocolate, granulated chicken broth, and coffee. Simmer uncovered for 10 hours on the lowest possible heat. If needed, add more beef broth to keep the level from boiling down.
7. Mix the masa harina with water until a slurry forms. Stir into the chili and simmer until thickened.
8. Chili is best when it has had time to age, at least a week. Serve in bowls with hot, fresh flour tortillas.
Compliments of Garry's Home Cookin'
